title: Felted merino slippers
subtitle: Seamless slippers shaped around a foot-shaped resist
excerpt: Cut a foot-shaped resist from dense foam, wrap it in cross-hatched layers of merino roving, wet-felt until firm, then slide the resist out and full the slippers to fit. The resist method produces a seamless slipper with a rounded toe and no stitching.
sourceNotes: Felted slippers via the resist method are a well-established craft. The foot-resist approach avoids the seam at the toe that the flat-slab slipper construction requires. Method is observation-led.

content.text: These slippers are made over a dense-foam foot-shaped

cut around a tracing of the wearer's foot. The resist method produces a completely seamless slipper: no stitching at the toe, no gusset, no joined seams that can wear through. Allow about 20% extra size in the resist for shrinkage during
: if the foot measures 26 cm long, the resist should be 31-32 cm long.

attrs.body: Make both slippers at the same time on two resists cut from the same tracing. Merino typically shrinks 20-25% during felting; Corriedale shrinks slightly less. Felt the slippers to a size slightly larger than the final target foot, then wear them briefly while damp on the final fulling pass to fit them exactly.
attrs.title: Sizing note

items.qty: 200 g for a UK size 6-7 pair
items.name: Merino roving (one or two colours)
Dense foam rubber, 2 cm thick (for the resist)

items.qty: in a large bowl or bucket
items.name: Hot water (60-65°C)

items.qty: for cutting the resist and the opening
items.name: Sharp scissors

content.text: Trace around one foot on paper, adding 20% to the length (so 26 cm becomes 31-32 cm) and 15% to the width. Cut this template out and trace it twice onto the dense foam. Cut the foam resists cleanly with scissors. Mark left and right on the foam so you keep them paired through the session.
Step 2: Lay and felt each slipper

Work one slipper at a time. Lay the resist flat. Cover the top face with three layers of roving in cross-hatched directions, wrapping the roving 5 cm over the edge onto the underside. Wet and partially felt the first face before carefully flipping and laying three more layers on the underside, again wrapping over the edge. Wet, soap, and rub both faces alternately for 30-40 minutes until both pass the pinch test.
Step 3: Remove the resist and cut the opening

Slide the foam resist toward the top of the slipper and cut a horizontal slit across the felt at the ankle position. Remove the resist. Trim the opening to a clean oval that will fit over the ankle. Rub the cut edge firmly with soapy fingers to bind the raw fibres.
Step 4: Full to size and fit

Continue fulling the empty slipper: roll in the bamboo mat for 3-4 minutes, turn, roll again. Check the size against the foot every 5 minutes by slipping the damp slipper onto the foot. When the slipper is about 5% larger than the final target size, stop fulling, rinse, and dry flat. As it dries it will shrink the final 5%. If it fits too tightly while still damp, hold the opening open and steam briefly (over a kettle or pot of simmering water) to relax the fibres before re-shaping.

attrs.intro: Two problems specific to slipper making.

items.fix: Full the smaller slipper less in future. If the difference is minor (under 1 cm), the slippers will often fit the same after the final wearing-while-damp pass. For a significant difference, more fulling of the larger slipper may bring it back into range.
items.cause: One slipper was rubbed more vigorously or for longer than the other.
items.symptom: Slippers have felted to different sizes

items.fix: While the slipper is still damp, pull a small amount of roving of the matching colour and press it over the thin area. Rub with soapy fingers to felt it in. For small thin patches this is a reliable repair.
items.cause: Uneven layer thickness in the initial layout, or a point where the roving was pulled too thin when wrapping over the resist edge.
items.symptom: Slipper developed a thin patch or hole during felting

attrs.heading: Common problems
